title: STANDARD FOR FOLLOW-UP FORMULA FOR OLDER INFANTS AND PRODUCT FOR YOUNG CHILDREN* CXS 156-1987

sections.section: STANDARD FOR FOLLOW-UP FORMULA FOR OLDER INFANTS AND PRODUCT FOR YOUNG CHILDREN* CXS 156-1987
sections.summary: Title and revision history for the Standard for Follow-up Formula for Older Infants and Product for Young Children (CXS 156‑1987), adopted 1987 and amended 1989, 2011, 2017 and revised 2023, with a note that food additive provisions have been aligned and moved to the GSFA (CXS 192‑1995).

Preamble stating the Standard is divided into Section A (follow-up formula for older infants) and Section B (products for young children) and that application should be consistent with national/regional health and nutrition policies and legislation, taking into account the International Code of Marketing of Breast‑milk Substitutes and relevant WHO/WHA guidance.

sections.section: SECTION A: FOLLOW-UP FORMULA FOR OLDER INFANTS
sections.summary: Section A covers follow‑up formula for older infants and sets out definitions plus compositional, quality, safety, labelling, analytical and sampling requirements that products must meet to be presented as follow‑up formula for older infants.

Scope: this section applies to follow‑up formula for older infants (liquid or powdered), establishes compositional, quality, safety, labelling, analytical and sampling requirements, and states only products complying with these criteria may be presented as follow‑up formula for older infants.

Description: defines follow‑up formula as a breastmilk‑substitute used as the liquid part of the diet when complementary feeding is introduced, requires processing by physical means and packaging to prevent spoilage/contamination under normal handling, and defines 'infant' (≤12 months) and 'older infant' (6–12 months).

Product definition: follow‑up formula for older infants is manufactured as a breastmilk‑substitute to be used as the liquid part of the diet for older infants and must be processed only by physical means and packaged to prevent spoilage and contamination under normal conditions of handling, storage and distribution.
Other definitions: 'infant' means a person of not more than 12 months of age and 'older infant' means a person from 6 months up to 12 months of age.

Sets essential composition and quality requirements: follow‑up formula must be based on milk or other safe ingredients, provide 60–70 kcal/100 ml when prepared per manufacturer instructions, and meet specified nutrient minimums, maximums or guidance upper levels (GULs) per 100 kcal together with specific constraints (e.g., banned partially hydrogenated oils, limits on certain fatty acids); GULs are guidance values and may be adjusted with scientific or technological developments.

Details essential composition: follow‑up formula shall be based on milk or other proven safe ingredients, provide 60–70 kcal/100 ml when prepared, and contain specified nutrients per 100 kcal with defined minima, maxima and GULs; includes technical notes on protein calculation (N×6.25), amino acid requirements and permitted use of isolated L‑amino acids, and allows alternative protein minima or lower protein levels for certain protein bases subject to safety assessment by competent authorities.

sections.section: 3.1.1  Follow-up formula for older infants means a product, manufactured for use as a breastmilk -substitute, as a liquid part of a diet for older infants when progressively diversified complementary feeding is introduced.
sections.summary: Composition requirement: follow‑up formula must be based on cow or other animal milk or safe suitable ingredients and its nutritional safety and adequacy must be scientifically demonstrated to support older infants' growth and development.

sections.section: 3.1.2 When prepared ready for consumption in accordance with the instructions of the manufacturer, the products shall contain per 100 ml not less than 60 kcal (251 kJ) and not more than 70 kcal (293 kJ) of energy.
sections.summary: Energy requirement: when prepared ready for consumption per manufacturer instructions, follow‑up formula shall provide not less than 60 kcal (251 kJ) and not more than 70 kcal (293 kJ) per 100 ml.

sections.section: 3.1.3 Follow-up formula for older infants prepared ready for consumption shall contain per 100 kcal (100 kJ) the following nutrients with the following minimum and maximum or guidance upper levels (GULs) i  as appropriate.
sections.summary: Nutrient specification: follow‑up formula prepared ready for consumption must contain specified nutrients per 100 kcal (100 kJ) with the minimums, maximums or guidance upper levels (GULs) indicated in the standard.
Biotin requirement: follow‑up formula must contain at least 1.5 µg biotin per 100 kcal (0.36 µg/100 kJ) with a guidance upper level of 10 µg/100 kcal (2.4 µg/100 kJ).
Iron requirement: follow‑up formula shall contain 1.0–2.0 mg iron per 100 kcal (0.24–0.48 mg/100 kJ).
Calcium requirement: follow‑up formula shall contain a minimum of 50 mg calcium per 100 kcal (12 mg/100 kJ) with a guidance upper level of 180 mg/100 kcal (43 mg/100 kJ).

Phosphorus requirement: follow‑up formula shall contain at least 25 mg phosphorus per 100 kcal (6 mg/100 kJ) with a GUL of 100 mg/100 kcal (24 mg/100 kJ), and the GUL is intended to accommodate higher needs for formulas based on soy protein isolate.
Required calcium-to-phosphorus ratio in follow‑up formula: between 1:1 and 2:1.
Magnesium requirement: follow‑up formula shall contain at least 5 mg magnesium per 100 kcal (1.2 mg/100 kJ) with a guidance upper level of 15 mg/100 kcal (3.6 mg/100 kJ).
Specifies sodium content limits for the product: 20–60 mg per 100 kcal (4.8–14 mg per 100 kJ); no GUL listed.
Specifies chloride content limits for the product: 50–160 mg per 100 kcal (12–38 mg per 100 kJ); no GUL listed.
Specifies potassium content limits for the product: 60–180 mg per 100 kcal (14–43 mg per 100 kJ); no GUL listed.
Requires a minimum manganese content of 1.0 µg per 100 kcal (0.24 µg per 100 kJ) and provides guidance upper limits (GUL) of 100 µg per 100 kcal (24 µg per 100 kJ).
Requires a minimum iodine content of 10 µg per 100 kcal (2.4 µg per 100 kJ) and a GUL of 60 µg per 100 kcal (14 µg per 100 kJ).
Requires a minimum selenium content of 2 µg per 100 kcal (0.48 µg per 100 kJ) and a GUL of 9 µg per 100 kcal (2.2 µg per 100 kJ).
Requires a minimum copper content of 35 µg per 100 kcal (8 µg per 100 kJ) and provides a GUL of 120 µg per 100 kcal (29 µg per 100 kJ).
Requires a minimum zinc content of 0.5 mg per 100 kcal (0.12 mg per 100 kJ) and provides a GUL of 1.5 mg per 100 kcal (0.36 mg per 100 kJ).

Allows addition of optional ingredients to follow‑up formula for older infants only when their safety and suitability at the intended use level are demonstrated by generally accepted scientific evidence, the formula contains sufficient amounts to achieve the intended effect (considering human milk), and specified substances may be added in conformity with national legislation subject to the per‑100 kcal (100 kJ) limits provided (the list is illustrative, not exhaustive).

General purity requirements: all ingredients must be clean, good quality, safe and suitable for older infants and conform to normal quality attributes; vitamin compounds and mineral salts should be chosen from the Advisory Lists (CXG 10-1979) and any sodium derived from them must comply with the sodium limit in Section 3.1.3(e).
All ingredients must be clean, of good quality, safe and suitable for ingestion by older infants and conform to normal quality requirements such as colour, flavour and odour.

Vitamin compounds and mineral salts used as permitted must be selected from the Advisory Lists for foods for infants and young children (CXG 10-1979), and sodium contributed by these ingredients must remain within the sodium limit in Section 3.1.3(e).

sections.section: 3.3.2.1 Vitamin compounds and mineral salts used in accordance with Sections 3.1.3 (d) and (e) and 3.2.1 should be selected from the Advisory Lists of Nutrient Compounds for Use in Foods for Special Dietary Uses Intended for Infants and Young Children (CXG 10-1979). 4
sections.summary: Requires that vitamin compounds and mineral salts used under the referenced provisions be selected from the Advisory Lists of Nutrient Compounds for Use in Foods for Special Dietary Uses Intended for Infants and Young Children (CXG 10-1979).

sections.section: 3.3.2.2 The amounts of sodium derived from vitamin and mineral ingredients shall be within the limit for sodium in Section 3.1.3 (e).
sections.summary: Mandates that sodium contributed by vitamin and mineral ingredients must be within the sodium limit specified in Section 3.1.3(e).
When prepared according to directions for use, the product must be free of lumps and large, coarse particles.
Prohibits treatment of the product and its components by ionizing radiation.

Permits certain additives (acidity regulators, antioxidants, emulsifiers, packaging gases, thickeners) in accordance with Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) for food category 13.1.2 and prohibits flavourings; carry‑over of additives is allowed only if the additives are listed for category 13.1.2 or in the Advisory Lists and meet the stated conditions. The carry‑over conditions are that the additive level in raw materials/ingredients does not exceed the specified maximum and the food does not contain more of the additive than would be introduced under good manufacturing practice.

sections.section: 4.1 Acidity regulators, antioxidants, emulsifiers, packaging gases and thickeners used in accordance with Table 1 and Table 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) 1  in food category 13.1.2 (Follow-up formulae) are acceptable for use in foods conforming to this standard.
sections.summary: Accepts use of acidity regulators, antioxidants, emulsifiers, packaging gases and thickeners in this product only as listed in Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) for food category 13.1.2 (Follow‑up formulae).
States that no flavourings are permitted in this product.

Allows presence of food additives in the product by carry‑over only if they are listed in food category 13.1.2 of CXS 192-1995 or in the Advisory Lists (CXG 10-1979). The carry‑over is permitted only if (a) the additive amount in the raw materials/ingredients does not exceed the specified maximum and (b) the final food does not contain the additive in greater quantity than would be introduced under good manufacturing practice.
Products covered by this standard must comply with the Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Hygiene provisions recommend that products be prepared and handled in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and other relevant Codex codes, and that products comply with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997.

sections.section: 6.1 It is recommended that the product covered by the provisions of this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), 6 and other relevant Codex texts such as the Code of Hygienic Practice for Powdered Formulae for Infants and Young Children (CXC 66-2008), 7  and in the case of liquid formula that has been commercially sterilized should also consider the appropriate sections of the Code of Hygienic Practice for Aseptically Processed and Packaged Low-acid Foods (CXC 40-1993) 8 and the Code of Hygienic Practice for Low and Acidified Low-acid Canned Foods (CXC 23-1979), 9  as applicable.
sections.summary: It is recommended that the product be prepared and handled following the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and other relevant Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice, with additional specific guidance for commercially sterilized liquid formulae.

sections.section: 6.2 The products should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997). 10
sections.summary: Products should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).

For ready-to-eat products the fill of container must be at least 80% v/v for products <150 g, 85% v/v for 150–250 g, and 90% v/v for >250 g, measured as a percentage of the container's water capacity (volume of distilled water at 20 °C the sealed container holds when completely filled).

Labelling of follow-up formula for older infants must comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985), Guidelines on Nutrition Labelling (CXG 2-1985) and Guidelines on Nutrition and Health Claims (CXG 23-1997), and includes a prohibition on nutrition and health claims for infant foods except where specifically provided by Codex or national law, with detailed naming, ingredient, nutrition, date/ storage, use and additional labelling requirements set out in subsequent subsections.

Label text must be in the appropriate language(s), the product name shall be 'Follow-up formula for older infants' or an appropriate equivalent, and the sources of protein must be clearly declared with specified labelling options when the protein is exclusively from a named animal or plant source.

A complete list of ingredients must be declared on the label in descending order of proportion, except added vitamins and minerals may be grouped, and specific names must be given for ingredients of animal or plant origin and for food additives with their functional classes (INS number optional).

Nutrition information must declare energy (kcal and/or kJ) and grams of protein, carbohydrate and fat per 100 g or per 100 ml as sold and per 100 ml ready for use; the total quantity of each required vitamin and mineral (and other listed ingredients) per 100 g or per 100 ml as sold and per 100 ml ready for use; and may additionally declare these nutrients per 100 kcal or per 100 kJ.

Date marking and storage instructions must follow Section 4.7 of the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985), and where practicable storage instructions should be placed close to the date marking.

Labels must provide clear preparation and handling instructions: ready-to-use liquids used directly; concentrated liquids and powders prepared with potable or previously boiled water; instructions on storage, disposal of leftover product and warnings about health hazards, accompanied by clear graphic preparation instructions, and must state the product is not to be introduced before six months, is not a sole source of nutrition, and complementary foods should be provided.

Labels must not discourage breastfeeding and each container must carry a prominent message including 'Important notice', a statement that 'Breastmilk is the best food for your baby', advice that the product should be used only on health worker advice, and that use should not lead to cessation of breastfeeding; additionally, labels must have no pictures or representations that idealize use, suggest use under six months, promote bottle feeding, compare to breastmilk, or convey endorsements (unless approved), must not use terms such as 'humanized' or 'maternalized', and must be distinctly labelled to avoid confusion with infant formula or other young-child products and must not reference those other products.

Methods of analysis and sampling for checking compliance with this standard shall be those contained in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to the provisions in this standard.
